# Discussion

## Principal findings

This study presents Robust-Lit-Review, the first end-to-end pipeline that automates the full systematic review workflow from topic formulation through submission-ready manuscript generation. Unlike existing tools that address isolated stages of the review process, our system integrates literature search, screening, data extraction, narrative synthesis, PRISMA compliance auditing, and manuscript formatting into a single reproducible pipeline. Validation across three hematology/oncology case studies---hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis, diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, and relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma---demonstrated consistent performance, with generated manuscripts ranging from 10,000 to 13,000 words and achieving 33--35 out of 36 applicable PRISMA 2020 checklist items [@Page2021PRISMA].

A key technical finding is that LLM-based extraction using Claude Haiku substantially outperformed regular expression-based extraction, achieving approximately 70% accuracy compared to 42% for regex approaches. This result underscores the advantage of natural language understanding over pattern matching when extracting heterogeneous clinical data from unstructured abstracts, where study designs, outcome reporting conventions, and terminology vary widely across publications. The modular parallel architecture proved essential for managing context window constraints: by decomposing the review into independent extraction and synthesis tasks that operate on subsets of the literature, the pipeline avoids the context overflow problem that would arise from attempting to process dozens or hundreds of abstracts in a single LLM call.

## Comparison with existing approaches

The systematic review automation landscape has evolved rapidly, yet no prior tool has closed the full loop from search to manuscript. ASReview employs active learning to prioritize records during screening, substantially reducing reviewer workload, but produces no structured extraction or narrative output [@vandeSchoot2021ASReview]. Otto-SR extends automation further by combining LLM-powered screening with structured data extraction, achieving high sensitivity and accuracy, but stops short of manuscript generation [@ottoSR2025]. LitLLM addresses the writing stage by generating draft literature review text from retrieved references, but assumes that search, screening, and quality assessment have already been completed by other means [@LitLLM2024]. Covidence and Rayyan provide collaborative screening platforms with varying degrees of AI assistance but do not automate synthesis or reporting [@Covidence2024; @Ouzzani2016Rayyan].

Two recent survey articles published in this journal comprehensively catalogued the AI-assisted systematic review tool landscape, identifying capabilities and gaps across dozens of platforms [@Ge2024Leveraging; @Khalil2024Automation]. Our work moves beyond surveying the field to building and validating a complete system that addresses the gap these surveys identified: the absence of an integrated pipeline with built-in quality assurance. Critically, Robust-Lit-Review incorporates a PRISMA self-audit loop, inspired by reflexion-based self-repair [@Shinn2023Reflexion], that iteratively evaluates and repairs manuscript compliance---a feature absent from all prior tools.

## The hallucination challenge

The application of LLMs to evidence synthesis raises a fundamental concern: large language models can fabricate statistics, invert the direction of findings, and attribute claims to incorrect sources. In a domain where clinical decisions may depend on the accuracy of synthesized evidence, hallucination is not merely an inconvenience but a potential patient safety issue.

Our pipeline implements several mitigation strategies. First, structured extraction operates directly on real abstracts retrieved from OpenAlex [@Priem2022OpenAlex], constraining the LLM to summarize information that is present in the source text rather than generating claims from parametric knowledge. Second, the PRISMA audit loop uses an LLM-as-judge approach [@Zheng2024Judging] to evaluate each checklist item against the generated manuscript, flagging non-compliant sections for automated repair. Third, checkpoint CP5 requires human verification of key clinical claims against source abstracts before the manuscript proceeds to final formatting.

These mitigations reduce but do not eliminate hallucination risk. Automated grounding checks---in which extracted claims are systematically cross-referenced against source text using entailment or similarity models---represent a promising direction but are not yet implemented. We emphasize that the current pipeline positions human-in-the-loop review as a safety net for catching residual errors, not as a bottleneck that negates the efficiency gains of automation. The goal is to shift the human role from laborious manual work to focused quality verification.

## Ethical considerations

Transparency is a core design principle. All pipeline code is open source, all LLM prompts are disclosed in the repository, and every decision point is logged in a checkpoint file that enables post-hoc auditing. This commitment to openness addresses growing concerns in the research community about the reproducibility of AI-assisted research outputs.

On the question of authorship, we take the position that the pipeline assists human judgment but does not replace it. Human investigators define the research question, set inclusion and exclusion criteria, review checkpoint outputs, and make final editorial decisions. The LLM components function as sophisticated research assistants, not autonomous authors. This framing aligns with guidance from the International Committee of Medical Journal Editors and publisher policies requiring disclosure of AI-generated content.

Reproducibility is supported by the pipeline's checkpoint architecture: the combination of pipeline state files, intermediate outputs, and the checkpoint log enables exact replication of any generated review, given the same input parameters and model versions. However, we acknowledge that LLM outputs exhibit inherent stochasticity, and exact token-level reproduction requires fixed random seeds and model version pinning.

We also recognize a potential source of bias: journal quality filtering based on h-index thresholds (h-index of 100 or greater for Q1 classification) may systematically exclude important work published in journals from low-resource settings. This is a known limitation of bibliometric quality proxies and merits careful consideration when applying the pipeline to topics where the evidence base spans high- and low-resource contexts.

## Strengths

Several features distinguish this pipeline from prior work. It is fully open source and designed for reproducibility, with no proprietary dependencies for core search functionality---OpenAlex provides free, unrestricted access to bibliographic metadata [@Priem2022OpenAlex]. Validation across three case studies spanning distinct hematology/oncology domains---a rare inflammatory syndrome, an aggressive lymphoma, and a relapsed plasma cell neoplasm---provides evidence of generalizability within the field. The PRISMA self-audit with automated repair represents a novel application of reflexion-based quality assurance [@Shinn2023Reflexion] to manuscript generation. The modular architecture allows each component---search, screening, extraction, synthesis, and auditing---to be independently replaced or upgraded as better models and methods become available.

## Limitations

Several limitations warrant acknowledgment. First, validation has been conducted exclusively in hematology/oncology; generalizability to other medical specialties, the social sciences, or engineering domains remains unconfirmed and cannot be assumed. The clinical vocabulary, study design conventions, and outcome reporting patterns in hematology/oncology may not transfer to fields with different methodological traditions.

Second, the pipeline currently operates on abstracts only, without full-text PDF parsing. While abstracts provide sufficient information for many extraction tasks, nuanced data---such as subgroup analyses, adverse event details, and methodological quality indicators---often reside exclusively in full-text manuscripts. This limitation constrains the depth of extraction achievable by the current system.

Third, the pipeline processes only English-language publications, potentially excluding relevant evidence published in other languages. Fourth, formal meta-analysis capability---including effect size calculation, heterogeneity assessment, and forest plot generation---is not yet implemented, limiting the pipeline to narrative synthesis. Fifth, hallucination detection at checkpoint CP5 relies entirely on human review; without automated grounding verification, subtle fabrications could escape detection if reviewer attention lapses. Sixth, citation accuracy depends on the quality of BibTeX records returned by source APIs, which may contain formatting errors or incomplete metadata. Finally, the journal quality thresholds used for filtering (such as h-index of 100 or greater for Q1 classification) are approximate heuristics rather than validated cutoffs.

## Future directions

Several extensions would substantially enhance the pipeline's capabilities. Living review functionality, in which the pipeline periodically searches for new articles published since the last run and integrates them into an updated synthesis, would address the obsolescence problem inherent in traditional systematic reviews. Citation graph intelligence, leveraging the referenced_works field in OpenAlex, could identify seminal papers and trace evidence networks to improve search comprehensiveness. Full-text PDF parsing would enable richer extraction context, particularly for methodological quality assessment and detailed outcome data. Automated grounding checks using entailment models or PubMedBERT-based semantic similarity [@PubMedBert2023] would provide a scalable verification layer between LLM-generated text and source documents. Multi-language support and integration with PROSPERO registration workflows would broaden applicability and enhance methodological rigor. These developments, taken together, would move the field closer to a vision of continuously updated, globally inclusive, and fully verifiable automated evidence synthesis.
